data.title: JELD-WEN Holding: Cheap, But Navigating Considerable Headwinds
data.description:
Shares of door and window manufacturer JELD-WEN Holding, Inc. (NYSE:JELD) have fallen 40% since reporting a big Q3 2024 miss in November 2024 and downwardly revising its FY 2024 outlook. Industrywide demand is weak, although the company has been hurt by its quality and delivery issues, as well as the loss of a retail client to a Chinese manufacturer in Q3 2024. In the middle of a transformation that saved it $115 million in costs during FY 2024 but will continue to require significant capex spending, the recent beneficial owner buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis/recommendation follows below.

Company Overview

JELD-WEN Holding, Inc. is a Charlotte, North Carolina-based designer, manufacturer, and distributor of doors, windows, and related building products primarily for markets in North America and Europe. These offerings are produced and dispensed out of 79 facilities in 15 countries, having divested of its Australasian operations in 2023. JELD-WEN was formed in 1960, effectively controlled by private equity in 2011, and went public in 2017, raising net proceeds of $472.8 million at $23 per share. Its stock trades around $8.75 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $750 million.

Products

The company is primarily known for its doors, including interior, exterior, and patio, which accounted for 63% of its FY 2023 net revenue. Windows contributed 21% of the total, while ancillary products, such trim board, glass, hardware, locks, window screens, and molded door skins, were responsible for 16% of JELD-WEN’s top line.

Operating Segments

Although its products are sold into ~71 countries, the preponderance of the company’s net revenues are derived from its two geographic operating segments: North America and Europe.

Providing 72% of the company’s top line is North America. It consists primarily of sales of wood veneer, composite wood, steel, glass, and fiberglass residential doors and windows into the new construction, repair, and remodel markets in the U.S. and Canada. It provided Adj. EBITDA of $211.6 million on net revenue of $2.07 billion in the first nine months of 2024 (YTD 2024) versus Adj. EBITDA of $288.0 million on net revenue of $2.38 billion in YTD 2023, representing a decline of 27% and 13%.

In Europe, JELD-WEN is the only manufacturer capable of serving nearly every country on the continent. That said, its bailiwick is residential and non-residential doors in Germany, the UK, France, Austria, Switzerland, and Scandinavia. This geography provided YTD 2024 Adj. EBITDA of $24.5 million on net revenue of $811.3 million as compared to YTD 2023 Adj. EBITDA of $51.2 million on net revenue of $911.9 million in YTD 2023, representing drops of 52% and 11%.

Putting it altogether with corporate overhead, JELD-WEN earned $0.87 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $235.2 million from continuing operations on revenue of $2.88 billion in YTD 2024 versus $1.22 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $293.9 million from continuing operations on revenue of $3.28 billion in YTD 2023, representing declines of 29%, 20%, and 12%, respectively.

Operating and Share Price Performance

Owing to historically low interest rates, shares of JELD rebounded 416% off a pandemic-selloff low to $31.24 in February 2021. That said, despite being the beneficiary of a Goldilocks interest rate scenario, the company’s stock price was meaningfully lower than its all-time high set in February 2018 for two reasons.

First, it had been dogged by breach-of-contract and antitrust lawsuits from a former customer (and competitor) Steves and Sons, arising from a prior JELD-WEN merger. Initially filed in 2016, a jury found in 2018 that JELD-WEN had violated the Clayton Antitrust Act and the plaintiff was entitled to damages of $58.6 million. Even though the market didn’t react severely to the verdict at the time, it marked the beginning of a protracted decline in the company’s stock. After appeals and counter lawsuits spanning several years, it was adjudicated that JELD-WEN was to divest of its operations in Towanda, Pennsylvania during 2021. This marked the first time a private company (rather than a government agency like the FTC) had compelled a company to unwind a merger in a Circuit Court. That said, the matter is still ongoing, with JELD-WEN filing a motion to vacate all court orders requiring the divestiture in May 2024.

Also weighing on the stock was the exit of private equity, with Onex Corporation selling its remaining 31.9 million shares in three separate transactions during 2021. JELD-WEN questionably elected to be the buyer of 16.7 million of those shares.

Either way, after these events conspired to pressure JELD-WEN’s stock, sharply higher mortgage rates took over, sapping demand for new homes while trapping would-be sellers in theirs. This dynamic stunted any growth, with net revenues falling 5% from $4.54 billion in FY 2022 to $4.30 billion in FY 2023. Even though the price of its offerings rose by an average of 5%.

As the market was slowing, management embarked on a restructuring of its business that has resulted in $89.3 million of charges since the start of the initiative in 2023 that is forecast to drive $115 million of savings in FY 2024. Also, part of this initiative is an ongoing “right-sizing” of the manufacturing footprint that includes heavy capex investments in its more productive facilities.

When the company entered FY 2024, it anticipated generating Adj. EBITDA of $395 million on net revenue of $4.15 billion, representing an increase of 4% on a decrease of 3% at the top line – based on range midpoints.

Q3 2024 Financials & Revised Outlook

However, owing to continuing challenges that were elucidated in its Q3 2024 financial report, management was compelled to downwardly revise it FY 2024 outlook for a second time on November 4, 2024, after reporting earnings from continuing operations of $0.32 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $81.6 million on net revenue of $934.7 million versus $0.53 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $105.7 million on net revenue of $1.08 billion, representing declines of 40%, 23%, and 13%. The bottom line was $0.08 shy of expectations, while the top line was $55.9 million below consensus.

Weakness was relatively even across geography, with North America falling 14% and Europe down 12%. That said, JELD-WEN lost a Midwest window retailer client to the Chinese in the quarter that will impact FY 2024 net revenue by $20 million to $25 million and approximately four times that figure in FY 2025. That said, management characterized it as a “one-off” and not a trend.

Also, deteriorating conditions – especially in multifamily and higher-end home construction – as well as “quality and delivery challenges” were blamed for the dreadful performance versus expectations. Demand has dropped to a level where certain plants can’t justify two work shifts, rather one less productive stint littered with overtime. Somewhat in response to these circumstances and somewhat due to its ongoing restructuring, JELD-WEN announced that it was shuttering one plant in Alabama and one in Denmark, moving manufacturing to more productive facilities. Not only was volume down, but a shift to lower average selling price products was responsible for nearly half of the year-over-year decline.

These developments drove management to lower its guidance to Adj. EBITDA of $272.5 million on net revenue of $3.725 billion, below its original takes by 31% and 10%, respectively. And there is no end to the weakening, as the implied Q4 2024 year-over-year decline of 17% at the top line was steeper than the 12% drop experienced during YTD 2024 and 13% in Q3 2024. Although the company’s official FY 2025 guidance won’t be revealed until February 2025, it does expect the year to be “slightly down” versus FY 2024. On a positive note, any tariffs imposed by the incoming administration will have next to no impact on JELD-WEN.

Unsurprisingly, shares of JELD tanked 30% in the subsequent trading session to $9.92 and are now down 40% since its Q3 2024 financial report.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

In addition to Adj. EBITDA and net revenue, JELD-WEN revised its FY 2024 operating cash flow projection from $200 million to $125 million. As stated previously, the company continues to reinvest in new and updated plants, having outlaid $109.8 million in YTD 2024, after investing between $83.2 million and $98.3 million annually during FY 2021-FY 2023 and is planning capex of $175 million to $200 million in FY 2025. As such, it does not foresee repurchasing any more shares or paying a dividend anytime soon. As of September 30, 2024, JELD-WEN held cash and equivalents of $208.5 million (and total liquidity of $637 million) against debt of $1.21 billion, for net leverage of 3.1, up from 2.5 at the beginning of 2024.

With FY 2024 a massive disappointment from a business environment perspective, it isn’t surprising to see the Street unanimously negative on the company’s prospects. So far in 2025, a half dozen analyst firms, including Jefferies and Barclays, have either reiterated or downgraded JELD to a Hold or Sell ratings. Price targets proffered range from $7 to $10 a share. Potential tariffs have also been a headwind to building materials stocks over the past month.

On average, they expect JELD-WEN to earn $0.82 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $3.73 billion in FY 2024, followed by $1.03 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $3.59 billion in FY 2025.

Beneficial owner Turtle Creek Asset Management has used the recent pullback to cost average, adding over 450,000 shares on January 10th and January 13th, upping its ownership interest to roughly 18%.

Verdict

Although JELD-WEN Holding, Inc. management has made headway in its “transformation” with $115 million of cost savings in FY 2024, it is challenging to measure what is truly productive and what is a function of normal right sizing considering the deteriorating business backdrop. It is also challenging to envision a 4% drop at its FY 2025 top line translating to a 26% increase at its bottom line, like the Street analysts are currently forecasting. JELD-WEN has a line of sight on what it wants to accomplish, but not on a turnaround for its industry. Its stock trades at investible metrics of just over 8 times FY 2025E EPS and an EV/TTM Adj. EBITDA of 5.5, but the bet here is that the door and window business will continue to deteriorate into mid-2025, providing a lower entry point into JELD-WEN at some point in the future.

data.title: MarineMax Still Deserves A Bullish Outlook
data.description:
I have always found myself interested in odd companies. The things that make most investors excited, like cutting-edge technology, don't really do it for me. But one thing that does, even though I don't use one, is the boating space. One of the players in this market is none other than MarineMax, Inc. (NYSE:HZO). For those not familiar with the business, the company operates as the largest recreational boat and yacht retailer in the world. Well, back in May of last year, I ended up reaffirming the company as a “strong buy” candidate.

This was based on how shares of the company were priced. Since then, we have seen fundamentals change quite a bit. Revenue has dropped, and profitability metrics have come in mixed. For this year, it's likely that financial performance will be slightly better than it was last year. But after seeing the stock rise 13.3% since my previous article on it, and even though shares are actually cheaper than most other similar firms, I do think that downgrading it to a “buy” makes sense.

A look at recent figures

According to the management team at MarineMax, the company is a global leader in all things related to the recreational marine market. The business has 75 boat dealerships that sell over 30 exclusively marketed boat brands to customers who want them. The enterprise also has a portfolio of over 65 marina and storage locations spread across no fewer than 15 countries. Even though the company prioritizes the sale of boats, it is engaged in multiple aspects of the space, including the boat brokerage industry, the finance, and insurance market, the provision of super yacht services, and even manufacturing.

When I last wrote about MarineMax in May of last year, we had data covering through the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Results now extend through 2024 as a whole. Hence, it may be a good idea to start with overall performance for 2024 as a whole. During that year, revenue for the company came in at $2.43 billion. That represented growth of 1.5% compared to the $2.39 billion the business generated one year earlier. Of the $36.3 million increase in revenue that the company enjoyed year over year, about $6.6 million was because of acquisitions and new locations that the business opened. The rest of the rise, $29.4 million in total, was attributed to comparable store sales growth that management claimed was because of both new and used boat revenue.

This growth in revenue was driven entirely by the company’s Retail Operations segment. Overall revenue there increased by 5.4%. However, there was weakness elsewhere that negatively impacted results. The Product Manufacturing segment is the sole culprit here. This is the part of the company that produces sports yachts under the Cruisers Yachts brand name. These are usually models that are between 33 feet and 60 feet in size. However, the company does have another brand under this umbrella called Intrepid Powerboats that, as its name suggests, focuses on the powerboat market. Year over year, this segment saw revenue decline an astounding 30.4% from $222.3 million to $154.8 million.

Unfortunately, the industry for recreational boating is rather challenged at the moment. During the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, demand for boats shot up. People were focusing on social distancing and initially had extra cash to work with. It helped that interest rates had come down rather significantly. In fact, when it comes to the US recreational boating industry, management said that it peaked at $59.3 billion in 2022. But by 2023, it had dipped to $57.7 billion. According to one other source, 2024 saw even lower revenue of $55 billion. However, that same source also estimates that the US market will see growth this year of between 3% and 5%. Of course, given broader economic uncertainty and the prospect that interest rates will remain elevated for an extended period of time, we will see if this forecast does come to fruition.

Even though revenue increased from 2023 to 2024, net profits actually fell from $139.3 million to $38.1 million. Part of this was because of a rise in interest expense from 2.2% of revenue to 3.1%. That doesn't sound like much. However, it amounts to an extra $20.5 million year over year. This was because of rising interest rates and an increase in net debt from $758.6 million to $1.10 billion. It's also true that the company's gross profit margin contracted from 34.9% to 33%. This seems to have been driven by lower new and used boat margins, as the company was willing to accept lower pricing to push retail sales higher in an environment that management described as “soft.” On top of this, MarineMax experienced a rise in its selling, general, and administrative costs from 26.5% of revenue to 27.7%. This was because of inflationary pressures and acquisitions that the company has made.

Other profitability metrics ended up being negative. Adjusted net income, for instance, fell quite a bit from $116.8 million to $49.1 million. Operating cash flow did improve from negative $222.2 million to negative $25.7 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a fall from $188.8 million to $115.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company contracted from $239.5 million to $160.2 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, we have had data come in for a single quarter. During that time, revenue for the company hit $468.5 million. Even though the company benefited from a slight improvement in manufacturing revenue, it suffered from an 11% plunge in comparable store sales because of what management claimed were “challenging economic trends” and the residual impact associated with two major hurricanes.

On the bottom line, the firm did see an improvement in net income from $0.9 million to $18.1 million. A jump in its gross profit margin from 33.3% to 36.2%, caused by a favorable mix of product sales, a greater proportion of revenue coming from higher margin operations, and changes to the promotional environment for the company, ended up doing a lot of the heavy lifting. However, the firm also saw a 16.5% drop in selling, general, and administrative costs because of cost savings initiatives implemented in 2024 and because of some other miscellaneous factors.

Unfortunately, other profitability metrics were quite mixed. For instance, adjusted net profits actually contracted from $4.4 million to $4.1 million. Operating cash flow worsened from negative $89.1 million to negative $146.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we do get a nice rise from $9.6 million to $30.7 million. However, EBITDA for the company dipped ever so slightly from $26.6 million to $26.1 million.

Even management seems to acknowledge that there will be considerable uncertainty this year. They have gone so far as to say that adjusted earnings per share could be between $1.80 and $2.80. To put this in perspective, at the low end, it would mean net profits of $42.1 million. And at the high end, it would translate to $65.5 million. The midpoint here is $53.8 million. They also expect EBITDA to come in at between $150 million and $180 million. If we assume that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate as EBITDA at the midpoint, we would get a reading of $119.4 million.

In the chart above, we can see what all of this means for the valuation of the company. On a standalone basis, shares look reasonably attractively priced. This is particularly true when it comes to the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple. However, when I last wrote about the company in 2024, the forward estimates for that year implied a price to earnings multiple of 9.4, a price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple of 4.2, and an EV to EBITDA multiple of 7.7. So, relatively speaking, the stock has gotten more expensive. That is a big reason for my decision to downgrade it from a “strong buy” to a “buy.”

Having said that, shares are still cheap compared to most similar firms. In the table below, I compared it to five such businesses. On both a price to earnings basis and on a price operating cash flow basis, only one of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is. However, on an EV to EBITDA basis, three of the five companies ended up being cheaper.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
MarineMax 14.4 6.1 10.9
Brunswick Corporation (BC) 34.6 10.3 10.3
MasterCraft Boat Holdings (MCFT) 41.2 121.7 27.3
Marine Products Corporation (MPX) 18.2 10.3 12.2
Malibu Boats (MBUU) 13.9 12.6 7.2
BRP Group (DOOO) 22.0 4.1 8.2

Eventually, I fully expect MarineMax to do fine. After all, the company has a growth mindset and has done well to expand and create shareholder value. Since its humble origins back in 1998, the company purchased 35 different recreational boat dealers. It purchased five boat brokerages, six super-yacht service companies, and a wide array of other assets. 20 of these acquisitions have come since the end of 2019 and have brought on a combined annual revenue of $700 million. The additional scale the company has achieved, combined with the higher margin businesses it has picked up, such as those involving brokerage activities and storage and charter rentals, has also paved the way for margin expansion. Back in 2019, the business had a gross profit margin of only 15%. But last year, it was 23.7%.

Multiple times in its latest investor presentation, MarineMax has emphasized that it is still interested in delivering on growth and margin improvements through acquisition. And I see no reason why that would change any time soon. Management has also adopted an innovative mindset. Their investment strategy centers around looking for with a call “new wave innovations” and then using their presence to create additional scale to make growth in the marketplace in which the company operates value-add for shareholders. Even though the company is a major player in this market, it does have plenty of room to grow. According to one source, 93% of all US boat manufacturers are small businesses. And in the company’s latest quarterly report, it describes the retail boating industry as “highly fragmented.” This opens the door for countless additional acquisitions and the clustering of resources to boost profitability.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I have no choice but to remain at least slightly bullish on MarineMax. Recent financial performance has been disappointing in some respects, but upbeat in others. The stock is cheap on a relative basis and doesn't look bad on an absolute basis. Current estimates for 2025 indicate that this year will be at least slightly better than last year was. Add all of these things together, and I do believe that MarineMax deserves a “buy” rating at this time.

data.title: Beware, JPC Is Not Earning Its Income
data.description:
Introduction

The Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:JPC) is a closed-end fund that invests in exactly what its namesake says, preferred shares and opportunistic credit. The fund is over twenty years old and has returned well for shareholders over that time, with a total return of over 200%, and an annualized 5.46%.

The last year was a great year for JPC, with a near-24% total return, and very positive price movements, up nearly 12%.

The fund's tremendous run last year was mostly due to the price adjustment falling in line with the fund's NAV. It has previously traded at a fairly steep discount.

The discount that had emerged in late 2023 has evaporated, with the value now being at a 0% discount to NAV. This run from nearly 16% discount to 0% represents a 16% gain to shareholders not driven by the underlying investments at all. While this was a good thing for shareholders that bought at those levels, this may pose risks for new investors.

Distributions & Yield

JPC is an easy fund to write headlines for, typically, since it carries a high yield that can exceed 10% at times. When looking at past articles, most headlines are about the yield.

This makes it clear to me that the yield is a selling point for investors interested in JPC, and is something that I should very closely inspect for my own review of the fund.

The current yield is 9.6%, up from the lows of 7.5% in 2024 and even lower in 2021 and 2022 when it was closer to 6%.

These distributions are supposed to be fairly level, and they are, which is a positive. The fund has been paying a monthly distribution of $0.0665/share since July 2024, a boost from the previous $0.0475/share it was paying before that.

This chart may be misleading, as it appears that they missed the January distribution. They still had all twelve dividends for 2024, and the ex-date in December that would normally put a payment in January instead had an earlier payment date.

This distribution was made on December 31st, as opposed to a date in January. There were two distributions made in December, and the 12/31 dividend would normally have been paid in January if it followed the ordinary pattern of ex-dates and payment dates.

It's unclear to me why this was done by management, but it is clear that the fund did not miss any distributions; there instead was a scheduling change with the payment dates that pushed the January 2025 dividend to be paid out on 12/31/24.

This consistent dividend is a positive for most income investors, as they are often less concerned about the yield so long as the actual dividend is predictable. Looking back, we can see a lot of changes over the years in how much they pay out, but they tend to follow through with even distributions for at least a year at a time.

Note: the spikes in the chart below are special distributions made at the end of the year in both 2023 and 2024.

When looking deeper, I found trouble in paradise. The JPC UNII report, which is dated 12/31/24, notes that JPC is only earning a little more than 2/3rd of its distributions from investment income.

This means that there is a $0.0927 gap between what the fund earned and what it distributes. This money has to come from somewhere if they are not earning it, but still distributing the normal amount.

When looking at the past year's distributions, we see that since August, the fund has been distributing return-of-capital ("RoC"), which is essentially dividends funded by the sale of the underlying assets in the fund, or distributing the cash the fund has otherwise earmarked for investments.

This kind of RoC is typically considered "bad RoC" as it means that the fund has to eat itself to keep the distributions going. While this is a positive for income investors who need the exact $0.0665/share distribution and rely on the income, it is not a sustainable long-term practice.

Despite a 5.5% gain in price over the last six months, these distributions (shown with the "D" symbol in the chart above) seem to be eating at NAV and keeping it depressed. Remember that over this time, investors saw the discount revert as well, from a low of 4.86% to 0%.

Discount

Speaking of the discount, it should be noted that we are in strange territory at 0%. Typically, the fund has traded at a discount, sometimes steeply, for most of its lifespan.

Buying in now, if the discount were to fall back again (as it historically has, but there are no guarantees), could cause shareholders to experience losses despite the underlying assets performing well. I caution investors to consider an investment carefully at these levels, and one must be willing to take on the risk of a 15%+ discount emerging at any time.

Holdings

So let's take a look under the hood and see what is generating the yield in the first place. If the fund has quality holdings, some investors may forgive the "bad RoC," assuming that it's temporary.

The portfolio is made up of 280 securities, with the majority being bonds and preferred shares, with very small allocations to convertible bonds and common stock.

This is to be expected given the fund's theme of preferred and opportunities. Among these holdings, the credit ratings are mostly distributed in the lower end of investment grade and the upper end of junk territory, with very little allocation to unrated securities.

Here is one of the very positive things I've seen about the fund, which is its geographic diversity. The fund is incredibly well diversified compared to most CEFs I review. The US is its second-largest holding, and the fund is fairly well distributed across the developed world.

This could provide a layer of protection from both fluctuations in USD, but also in the American markets more broadly. Most of the popular CEFs I cover on Seeking Alpha are US-only or US-focused funds, so adding JPC could serve to help diversify many portfolios.

The fund does have a lot of callable holdings, with only 3.2% of the fund's holdings not being callable. This could mean that some higher yield holdings could be called early, especially if rates suddenly fall for some unforeseen reason, like they did during the last two ZIRP periods.

Most of the holdings are fixed-to-fixed rate securities, meaning that they offer fixed rates that change on set schedules; unfavorable changes may be what has led to a disparity between earned income and the set distribution.

Leverage & Fees

We can't discuss CEFs without talking about these two, as they are what separates many CEFs from ETFs (and the discount/premium to NAV, of course).

JPC employs almost as much leverage as it is allowed to have (I believe 40% is the maximum legally allowed), sitting at 38.19% currently. I don't believe this is a bad thing, so long as the leverage carries an interest rate significantly below the prevailing rates of the debt they own. Since JPC focuses on credit BBB and below, this almost guarantees a 300-900bp gap between the cost of their leverage (assuming they are rolling it consistently) and the loans they own.

It does introduce risk, however, as a drop in the prices of these loans means they could affect JPC inordinately. If the whole bond market were to fall into turmoil, JPC will likely be impacted worse than other funds that do not employ leverage, or that employ significantly less leverage.

As far as fees are concerned, the fund carries a near 4% ER p.a., with the majority of that being attributed to leverage financing. The management fee alone is 1.22% for common shares of the fund.

I am not a big believer that fees should make or break a choice to invest, typically, as most fees can be justified if the returns are there. Note that all returns I show are net fees. It should be of note that JPC's fees are relatively average, with its 1.22% management fee falling between the CEF average of 1-1.5%.

Conclusion

The Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:JPC) presents as a fund that can help diversify an income portfolio, as it is geographically diverse compared to many other popular income-focused CEFs. While it can be a good tool to diversify corporate bonds and preferred stock exposure, there are some issues.

Namely, JPC is not earning its dividend, which is a red flag for me. It has not earned this increased dividend almost since it was announced, with only two months free of the "bad RoC," since then. This is a huge red flag if it continues, and could lead to future income reductions.

Secondly, the fund has typically traded at a discount and now is trading even, which could lead to losses for investors if they buy in now and then the fund goes back to trading at a discount again.

JPC has earned a hold from me and a recommendation to current shareholders to use caution when considering adding to this position. We are not in "sell" territory, but that doesn't mean we should be jumping to buy more or start a position now.

The phrase I like to use in these situations is "wait and see."

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Upstart: Great Q4 But Pricey, Given Model Uncertainties And High Take Rates
data.description:
Introduction

Upstart (NASDAQ:UPST) shares have been on a rollercoaster the last few years, peaking as high as $400 in October of 2021 and trading to lows of around $13 in January of 2023. After reporting Q4´24 on February 11th, shares look to have rallied to around $85 post-market. While conceptually feasible, I think shares look expensive given model uncertainties and high take-rates. The remainder of the article outlines Upstart's business, considers their AI-model based lending, reviews financials and valuations, and concludes.

Business

Upstart provides a platform or marketplace for lenders to connect with borrowers, selling the idea that older ways of deciding to provide credit, such as FICO scores, are suboptimal and that their AI model is substantially better at differentiating risk. Shares are valued on the idea that their AI model can be a significantly better risk differentiator than more traditional credit quality estimators. As such, Upstart viewed as a marketplace or platform for creditors and debtors suggests a TAM of around $3 trillion in annual loan originations.

So far, while venturing a little bit into auto loans, Upstart has yet to take any meaningful share of auto, home, or SBE loans. For now, it is fair to state that their TAM is around $149 billion and some part of the auto market, but could expand with new offerings. UPST earns its revenue through a fee for loan origination, although they hold some loans on their balance sheet, primarily for R&D purposes and also when they have been unable to make deals with capital partners.

Offering unsecured loans is a highly competitive business. If Upstart was considered a pure unsecured lending company, they would compete with traditional banks, SoFi (SOFI) and Pagaya (PGY), Affirm (AFRM), and LendingClub (LC), but also credit cards and Buy Now Pay Later offerings. On the credit-scoring side, Experian, Equifax, and TransUnion are competitors that have been in this game for a long time.

Upstart's ideal model is not to lend money, however. It is to provide creditors with a model that helps them to decide whom to provide loans to and at what rate - to originate loans and sell them to third parties such as lending partners. On this take, Upstart's model could be appealing to any lender for a small fee, and the company should be viewed more as a SaaS company with potentially great operational leverage that has temporarily had to be taken on loans on its balance sheet and some loans for the purposes of R&D. And that story looks good on paper. An astonishing 90% of Upstart loans are entirely automated - without a single person having to get involved. As of December 31, 2023, Upstart's model utilized a whopping 1,600 variables and has been trained on over 58 million repayment events.

In present reality, however, Upstart is currently a competitor to all of these companies issuing unsecured debt and credit score providers as well. Still, I will try to value Upstart not as a traditional lender, but more as a SaaS company that has the potential to disrupt the market for assessing credit risk.

The AI models

Upstart's selling point are its AI models that are used to assess credit quality better than traditional methods. Beyond models predicting the likelihood of loan default, Upstart has expanded their models to numerous other areas, such as models quantifying the risk of income and identity fraud. The first task of any valuation is to look into the conceptual feasibility of AI models versus traditional ways to assess credit quality, such as FICO scores. Immediately, there are some concerns with the AI model as a significant differentiator.

First, the historical performance of UPST's model has not instilled confidence. Upstart reported in their 10K'23 that "The quarterly vintages of core personal loans that originated in the first quarter 2021 through the second quarter 2023 are currently forecasted to underperform relative to their target returns". This represents ten quarters of expected underperformance.

The SEC in a comment letter from September 2024 asked Upstart to explain why recent vintage loans had underperformed: "Please tell us, with a view towards revised disclosure, the factors that led to lower than forecast performance for recent vintages, despite the access to additional data". In response, Upstart pointed to factors outside their control "including the elimination of government stimulus measures and the worsening of the macroeconomic environment, such as rising inflation and the resulting sharply higher interest rates."

The SEC also wanted to know why, as Upstart stated in their 10K'23, they expected loans originated in 2023 to revert to target level target performance. In the response, Upstart noted:

Our most recent AI models benefited from more data on borrower repayment patterns in the period of significant macroeconomic changes and resulted in substantive positive changes in observed and expected borrower repayments even for vintages with limited seasoning. This makes our more recent models more accurate than those used in early 2021.

Second, management talks about future prediction in a concerning manner. A model that predicts a future that has not yet occurred cannot be ex ante considered to be "more accurate". In numerous comments by management, it looks like they are talking about how taking account of more variables today makes the current models for the future more accurate. That, in my mind, is a fundamental misunderstanding about any model's predictions of the future.

Third, these models are necessarily permeated with subjectivity. Even if you could have predicted unemployment during Covid-19, your model would also have had to predict the unprecedented government support that led to debtors being able to continue to meet their payments. If the current model is fitted to the post-covid period of government aid, it would assume that unemployment does not increase loan defaults.

Adjusting for this requires human intervention and a subjective choice. This kind of subjectivity also affects data selection, what variables to include, and how to weigh those variables. This subjectivity can also affect other decisions. For example, Upstart cannot issue loans at 36%+ rates for regulatory reasons. In the Q2'24 earnings call, the CEO was asked how much Upstart's improvement in marketing efficiency in the quarter was due to approving more loans under 36%:

Partially, or most of it actually is due to model accuracy in the newest versions of the models, who are able to sort of identify more people who fit under that envelope of 36%, and the result of that is that you see marketing efficiency improving.

If Upstart's model says to approve no loans at all, then that would entail reporting a horrendous quarter. Coupled with subjective decisions, this can create incentives for subjective decisions that allow the model to take more risks.

As such, UPST's AI model is not a gift from god and suffers all sort of typical shortcomings regarding future predictions that any model, AI or not, has difficulties dealing with.

Financials and Valuation

My concerns with that model aside, it likely is better than competitors and is more accurate than traditional FICO scores at determining default rates. So let's look at Upstart as a pure SaaS business, ignoring the loans on its balance sheet. Of immediate notice is that Upstart's take rate looks really high. I calculate the take rate by dividing revenue from fees by transaction volume.

Revenues from fees are at around 10-12% of transaction volume, suggesting that Upstart's take rate of origination value is a whopping 10-12% for the service of providing an AI model and platform to lending partners, although declined to a low of 9.5% in Q4´24. Upstart has also engaged in risk sharing, which may limit the downside risk of lending partners, which could justify higher take rates.

This looks to be significantly more expensive than the competition, FICO scores, for example, only costs a couple of dollars. Let's exaggerate and say FICO scores costs around 2% of total loan origination. Then, Upstart would have to be 5-6x better at predicting credit quality than FICO to justify such high take rates. If you are skeptical of the AI model's ability to achieve superior outcomes on this scale, then on this basis alone, Upstart's take rate looks unreasonable. Not to mention they also compete with credit bureaus such as Experian, Equifax and TransUnion that can compete against accuracy by lowering take rates.

I conceived of Upstart's promise along the following lines: A simple platform where lenders and borrowers meet, powered by seamless AI that optimize rates, limit fraud, with significant operational leverage allowing for small take rates (made up by volume). This led me to think of modelling FCF along the following lines:

This model distributes Upstart's suggested TAM over varying loan amounts and assumes that the larger the loan, the less Upstart can take as a fee. On this model, they could compete with accuracy and operational leverage. This scenario could become reality, but given their current take rates, I think alternatives will be more appealing to lenders. More lending partners may join in, but that requires showing the potential of the model, which has not been entirely persuasive in recent years.

Even assuming the most generous scenario in the model above a 10% market share at a 30% FCF margin yielding $663 million in FCF annually, comes far off what looks baked into these shares:

This model uses analyst estimates for growth until 2026 and 18% annually thereafter. On these assumptions, the current valuation entails around $940 million in FCF in the terminal year and a 30% FCF margin for all years. Given the state of the business, I think this looks excessive on an absolute basis. On a relative basis, shares also look expensive (note, this is before the post-market increase of 25%):

I said I would not value Upstart as a lender, but as the market will likely attempt to value it as such, here is how expensive it presently is compared to lenders (also before the 25% move post-market):

Conclusion

The bull case for UPST is that they stop being restrained by a lack of lending partners, their models perform well, and they can take a decent fee at a high margin with automated loan origination and little overhead. The value proposition to creditors and debtors would be clear, and they can compete on margin by increasing volume. This scenario is likely what feeds into UPST's premium valuation compared to peers.

After reporting a stellar Q4'24, Upstart looks like they are on track toward realizing this model. Yet, I doubt the company will live up to its current premium. The AI model is questionable in itself, and even if workable, should only warrant a small fraction of the origination amount. Given Upstart's present take rate, I don't think their model's increased accuracy can compete against credit bureaus providing less accurate information but at a fraction of a cost. Any moderate to significant setback in the model's prediction would likely see share prices drop. Still, if they continue to post quarters like this, with higher volume, lower mean take rate, and without model hiccups, then shares look somewhat fairly valued. I am just not willing to bet on that.
